observations.managementAction: The recruitment process for the General Services Officer position is still ongoing, contributing to current limitations in workforce availability and time management.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the following:

a. For the Municipal Assessor to trace the historical cost of the identified lots of the Municipality.

b. To exhaust all possible means to document the legality of ownership for the 38 parcels of lot through a TCT in the name of the Municipality.

c. For the Municipal Assessor, the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile their records pertaining to Land account. Determine the acquisition cost of each land parcel composing the Land account.

d. In cases where a parcel was acquired through non-exchange transaction, such as donations, determine the prevailing fair value at the date of acquisition.

e. For the Municipal Accountant to record the land using the appropriate cost.
observations.managementAction: A review of prior years’ books of accounts revealed a recorded amount of ₱11,131,272.00 under the Land account. Management will initiate the retrieval of available documents and records to facilitate the reconciliation and verification of the account’s total valuation.

The Office of the Municipal Engineer have already conducted an inventory taking of the local roads of the municipality.

Reconciliation between the local road inventory and road networks that were recognized in the book of accounts and proper reporting on the physical count will be made.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the following: 

a. 	The Municipal Accountant to record purchased inventory on their appropriate asset account, use perpetual inventory method and for subsequent valuation, use weighted average method. Also, the MA should maintain a Supply Ledger Card for each commodity/stock. 

b. 	The Municipal Treasurer, in the absence of GSO, likewise maintain stock cards for supplies to account for the receipt and disposition of the same. The balance per stock card should always reconcile with the ledger cards of the accounting unit. 

c. 	Department Offices to use the RIS to record used inventory. This shall serve as monitoring of inventories requested and issued. 

d. 	The Municipal Treasurer and the Supply Officer to coordinate with the Department Offices in the consolidation of RIS and to prepare a weekly SSMI. 

e. 	Additionally, for the Municipal Accountant to use the SSMI as a basis of recording Inventories expense. 

f. 	Lastly, to require the Inventory Committee to conduct a periodic physical inventory of supplies or property. The physical count of inventory items by type shall be conducted semesterly and reported in the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI). This shall be submitted to the Auditor not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer already tapped the Local Chief Executive about the creation of the Inventory Committee and hiring of General Services Officer.
The automation of the payroll system has already been discussed and approved in the Sangguniang Bayan.
The management already adjusted a significant amount of the recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
1.7. We recommended that the:
a) Municipal Treasurer submit the complete list of taxpayers at the
beginning of the year to the Accounting Office as basis in accurately
recording of the accounts; and
b) Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant reconcile their records
to establish correctness of the recorded receivables at yearend.

2.24. Based on foregoing observations, we reiterated our audit recommendations to the Municipality that:
a) The Property Officer and the Accounting Office must prepare and
maintain complete Property Cards (PCs) and Property Ledger Cards
(PPELCs) and ensure that these are reconciled;
b) The Inventory Team prepare the RPCPPE in strict accordance with the
existing Manual and tag all PPE items with inventory stickers for easier
identification during inventory counts; and
c) The Accounting Office and Property Officer to conduct the necessary
preliminary activities for the cleanup of the Municipality’s PPE
accounts in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-06;

2.25. We also reiterated the prior year’s recommendations for the Property
Officer/GSO, Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer to coordinate
with each other and adhere to the provisions in maintaining the Local Road
Inventory and Local Road Network Property Card, in order to establish a
reliable basis for the amounts presented in the Financial Statements.

2.26. We further recommended that the Accounting Office review and correct its
practice in accumulation of costs and reclassification in relation to its
Construction-in-Progress Account.

3.7. We recommended that the:
a) Municipal Accountant recognize leave benefits yearly as they are earned,
rather than recording the entire liability upon the employee’s separation
in service;
b) Municipal Budget Officer establish an Employee Benefits Reserve Fund
on a yearly basis to ensure sufficient cash reserves for future monetization;
and
c) Human Resource Department coordinate with the Municipal Accounting
Office and Municipal Budget Office to ensure adherence to Rule XVI of
the Omnibus Rules Implementing Book V of Executive Order (EO) No.
292 on the yearly accounting and funding of leave benefits.

4.9. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a) recognize as current, liabilities that are expected to be settled in the
following year as required by IPSAS 1; and
b) render the unsubmitted Disbursement Vouchers and supporting
documents to facilitate the timely review of the above projects.

5.2. Accordingly, the Audit Team made the following recommendations a) the LCE
intruct the i) MPDO to prioritize PPAs so that specific dates or schedules of
implementation were indicated in AIP and ii) Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), BAC Secretariat, and Accounting Office to submit the lacking documents and explanation and/or justification on the observations noted.

5.26. We reiterated our recommendation that the:
a) MPDO prioritize PPAs so that specific dates or schedules of
implementation are indicated in AIP;
b) MPDO conduct prioritization of PPAs contained in the AIP so that
specific dates of implementation could be determined for the proper
implementation and monitoring of the same and avoid possible delays
during the execution of projects; and
c) Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and BAC Secretariat submit an
explanation or justification for the noted deficiencies in CY 2023 AAR.

5.27. We further recommended that the:
a) concerned officials plan judiciously and ensure that implemented projects are
aligned with medium – term plans;
b) Accounting Office review the payment for the dredging of various
waterways and effect adjustments if necessary;
c) concerned officials cause the formal constitution of clear guidelines on the
distribution of agricultural inputs for distribution; and
d) Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), BAC Secretariat, and other
concerned officials and employees submit necessary documents and/or
explanations for the audit observations on documentary deficiencies.

6.21. We reiterated that the:
a) Municipal Treasurer transfer the accurate amount of unexpended
LDRRMF to STF and GF;
b) Municipal Accountant
i. reconcile her records and effect adjusting entries when necessary;
and
ii. be cautious in the preparation of accounting entries and reporting of
LDRRMF so as not to mislead stakeholders and cause inappropriate
decisions;
c) concerned officers submit explanation or justification on noted
deficiencies stated in paragraph 6.18.

7.23. Based on the above observations, we recommended that:
a) the GFPS ensure that future GPBs and GAD AR are submitted on time
where PPAs in GPB are consistent with the GAD AR for proper
evaluation;
b) the GFPS in its subsequent changes to the GPB, notify and submit formal
notification to the DILG following the Circular.
c) the Municipality conduct assessments using the prescribed GFPS
Functionality Assessment tool to ensure the optimal functionality of its
GFPS and to address any identified gaps in compliance with GAD-related
mandates; and
d) the GFPS eventually adopt the application of the prescribed monitoring
and evaluation tools to determine how much of the Municipality’s actual
disbursements for implementing its major programs can be attributed to
GAD, seeking training opportunities from competent agencies to enhance
the gender-responsiveness of Municipality.
e) The GFPS furnish the Audit Team a reasonable justification on the over
expenditures mentioned in paragraph 1.10 with corresponding
supplemental budget or re-alignment, if there is any.

7.24. In respect of prior year audit recommendations, we also reiterate the
following:
a) The GFPS work on the passage of comprehensive guidelines on the
educational assistance program;
b) Submit justifications on the disbursements that exceeded the CY 2022
budgeted amounts, noted discrepancies in CY 2022 GAD AR, and CY
2023 over expenditure

8.40. Based on the observations regarding SEF compliance and utilization, We
recommended that:
a) the LSB ensure the future distribution of the copy of the SEF Budget to
the Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Accountant, and Municipal
Treasurer be promptly accomplished and shall be supported with proper
transmittal records from the concerned departments;
b) the LSB ensure that all future submissions of quarterly and annual
reports on SEF utilization are supported with proper transmittal records
or proof of receipt and be furnished to the proper concerned agencies
outlined in the Circular; and
c) the LSB in its future SEF budgets be prepared with more detailed and
itemized information to support transparency and effective evaluation
and monitoring.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor the following:
To create an Inventory Committee composed of an adequate number of members to complete the physical inventory in three months or less. The Inventory Committee shall have at least one member each from the Accounting and Property Divisions/Units of the agency.
Property Supply Officer:
The assignment of a property supply officer as he/she will update/ renew all the PARs/ AREs issued to end-users, and identify the properties issued to retired/ resigned employees/ officials that were not properly turned over. This would facilitate the identification of PPEs that are missing or not found in the supposed station, and to update the RPCPPE through the conduct of physical inventory of assets, ensuring to include all new acquisitions of PPEs and those assets that are not yet counted in the report;
Municipal Accountant:
To provide updated PPE Ledger Cards (PPELCs) to the Inventory Committee, including all newly acquired assets;
After the conclusion of activities for One-Time Cleansing, provide for the accounting entries for the recognition of PPEs found at station but not previously recorded, recognition of loss of PPEs, writing-off/ dropping from the books of accounts the remaining balances/ amounts lumped under the unreconciled SL of PPEs, and derecognition of non-existing/ missing PPEs (accomplish Annex D and also refer to Annex E of COA Circular 2020-006 for the entries)
Provide disclosures on PPE in the Notes to FS the total amount of non-existing/ missing PPEs included in the PPE balance, supported with breakdown as to sub-major PPE account group, and PPEs found station which are still needing appraisal, following item 9.0 of the same COA Circular;
Inventory Committee members:
Develop a Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) with assigned duties, cut-off dates, and a detailed schedule of inventory activities for approval by the Municipal Mayor.
Base the PIP on the latest RPCPPE, Inventory Count Form (Annex A), and PPELCs, in coordination with the Property and Accounting Units.
If no RPCPPE exists, use the ICF format (Annex A) and instruct all offices to submit lists of assigned PPEs; ensure inventory is conducted per the approved PIP, indicating each item’s condition.
For missing, unrecorded, or non-existent PPEs, follow COA Circular 2020-006 procedures.
With the Supply Officer’s assistance, accomplish Annexes A, B, and C of the same Circular.
Lastly, Disposal Committee shall be created pursuant to COA-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2024-1, dated January 30, 2024. This committee shall effect the disposal of PPEs as a result of the One-Time Cleansing of accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor take steps to transfer the buildings by formally requesting the Sangguniang Bayan (SB) to pass a resolution that authorizes the transfer of these assets to the designated recipient local government units and national agencies.
Furthermore, upon securing the SB resolution and necessary donation documents, the Municipal Accountant should prepare the appropriate adjusting entry to formally recognize the donation. To prevent an artificial inflation of expenses in the current year – which could distort financial performance and potentially result in an operating loss – the adjustment should not be recorded as a current-year expense. Instead, since these assets pertain to prior periods, they should have been classified as donations in previous years. We suggest closing the accounts under Prior Period Adjustments. The recommended adjusting entry is as follows:
Prior Period Adjustment						xxx
Accumulated Depreciation						xxx
Buildings 									xxx
School Buildings								xxx
Lastly, the Municipal Accountant should prepare restated financial statements to ensure comparability and consistency in financial reporting. These restated statements should accurately reflect the impact of the depreciation expenses recognized in previous years. This will enhance the reliability and transparency of financial information, enabling stakeholders to make well-informed comparisons across reporting periods and support financial decision-making.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to undertake the following actions to ensure proper accounting treatment, compliance with regulations, and transparency in financial reporting:
Initiate a request for tax credit or refund with the appropriate BIR Regional District Office, in accordance with the requirements set forth in Revenue Memorandum Circular No. 74-2024;
Municipal Accountant and all concerned offices to ensure the prompt and timely remittance of all tax obligations to the BIR. Timely tax payments are essential to avoid surcharges, interest, and compromise penalties that result in unnecessary government expenditure and potential accountability issues for the responsible officers.
Record the necessary adjusting entries to reflect the full receivable from the former accountant, as follows:
Dr. Due from Officers and Employees – FMR	    2,638,701.78
Cr. Accounts Receivable				               771,430.26
Cr. Due to BIR						1,867,271.52
In the event that the BIR grants the requested tax credit or refund, the approved amount (comprising the duplicate tax payments and the corresponding penalties) shall reduce the receivable recorded under “Due from Officers and Employees.” This adjustment shall reflect the portion of the previously recognized accountability that has been effectively relieved through the refund or credit, thereby ensuring accurate financial reporting and proper accountability.
Include a disclosure in the Notes to the Financial Statements presenting a detailed breakdown of the receivables related to the account, ensuring transparency and proper classification in accordance with applicable accounting and auditing standards.
Refrain from offsetting assets and liabilities, or income and expenses, unless the applicable accounting standards explicitly permit such offsetting. This is to ensure accurate presentation of financial information and compliance with the principles of faithful representation and comparability.

We reiterate our previous recommendation that the Municipal Mayor to instruct the following:
The Municipal Accountant to record purchased inventory on their appropriate asset account, use perpetual inventory method and for subsequent valuation, use weighted average method. Also, the MA should maintain a Supply Ledger Card for each commodity/stock.
The Municipal Treasurer, in the absence of GSO, likewise maintain stock cards for supplies to account for the receipt and disposition of the same. The balance per stock card should always reconcile with the ledger cards of the accounting unit.
All department offices shall use a RIS for every request of available inventory. This will serve as a means to monitor requested and issued inventory items.
The Municipal Treasurer to coordinate with the Department Offices in the consolidation of RIS and to prepare a weekly SSMI.
Additionally, for the Municipal Accountant to use the SSMI as a basis of recording Inventories expense.
Lastly, to require the Inventory Committee to conduct a periodic physical inventory of supplies or property. The physical count of inventory items by type shall be conducted semestral and reported in the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI). This shall be submitted to the Auditor not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
The Municipal Accountant to:
Prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to reclassify previously recorded PPE items valued below ₱50,000.00 as Semi-Expendable Properties, and recognize the corresponding expense through Prior Period Adjustments, reflected under the earliest Government Equity in accordance with COA Circular No. 2022-004.
Record all properties to be acquired with a value below ₱50,000.00 in their respective Semi-Expendable Property Account.
To enhance accountability and internal control over semi-expendable property, the Municipal Government shall ensure the proper preparation and maintenance of the prescribed forms, registries, and reports, as applicable, pursuant to Item 4.7 of COA Circular No. 2022-004 dated May 31, 2022:
These measures will ensure proper recording, monitoring, and accountability over semi-expendable property, reducing the risk of loss, misuse, or misstatement in the Municipality’s financial reports.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to direct the following:
The Municipal Treasurer should provide the Municipal Accounting Office with a detailed breakdown of advance receipts for RPT and SET at gross amount, including the corresponding discounts granted, to ensure accurate and complete recording in the books of accounts.
The Municipal Accountant should prepare restated financial statements to reflect the correct classification of advance payments by reclassifying amounts previously recorded under "Other Deferred Credits" to their appropriate accounts: "Deferred RPT and "Deferred SET." While an adjusting journal entry is not required since the entries were reversed in January 2025, prospective application of the correct accounting treatment is recommended to ensure consistency and compliance with applicable financial reporting standards moving forward.
The Municipal Accountant should strictly adhere to the prescribed accounting procedures for RPT and SET collections, to ensure that income, receivables, and other related accounts are fairly and accurately presented in the Municipality’s financial statements.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Municipal Treasurer or her representative to obtain a bank statement or bank snapshot at the end of the month. This will enable the Municipal Accountant to prepare the Bank Reconciliation Statements within 10 days after the month-end and submit the same to the Office of the Audit Team Leader.
Additionally, to record the adjustments, of any, in the General Ledger within 10 days after the end of each month.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the Municipal Treasurer to:
Review and Strengthen existing Internal Controls
Deposit all collections intact by the next banking day at all times.
Prohibit the use of collections for disbursements. Instead, utilize the petty cash fund.
Conduct a regular risk assessment to identify gaps in cash handling and deposit procedures and adopt corrective measures.
Enforce Segregation of Duties
Assign separate personnel for cash collection, disbursement, and reconciliation.
Conduct Regular Monitoring
Perform surprise cash counts and reconcile RCDs with bank statements.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor direct the following to the Municipal Treasurer:
Instruct all collecting officers to remit their collections and accomplish RCD at the close of each business day pursuant to Section 29 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.;
Ensure strict adherence to the existing cut-off period of 2:00 P.M. for remittances. All collecting officers should religiously follow this established protocol to prevent the accumulation of unremitted collections. By strictly following the current management procedures, accountability and the timely remittance of collections will be enhanced.
Conduct surprise cash counts and accountability checks on the accountable forms under the custody of collecting officers to ensure accuracy and proper handling of funds. These surprise audits is relevant to help detect any discrepancies or potential issues in a timely manner

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the Petty Cash Custodian to closely monitor the utilization of the petty cash fund and that replenishment is made on a monthly basis or when the petty cash level reaches 25% or as the need requires;
Follow Imprest System accounting policy in maintaining the Petty Cash Fund.
Proforma entries is illustrated below:

Dr.  Petty Cash Fund	xxx`
Cr. Cash in Bank	xxx
Incurred Expenses: No Journal Entry
Enter obligation in RAAOMO for the expenses incurred.
Replenishment of petty cash fund when it reaches 75% or as the need arises:
Dr.	Expenses incurred	xxx
Cr. Cash In Bank	xxx

We recommended that the Management carefully assess the maximum cash accountabilities of each Accountable Officers based on past transactions prior to application of fidelity bond in accordance with Sections 5.1 and 5.3 of Treasury Circular No. 02-2019 so that the interest of the Municipality is protected in case of loss or misapplication of public funds.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the management to:
Ensure full adherence to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002, particularly:
Section 4.4: Transfer all unutilized LDRRMF balances (e.g., Quick Response Fund and MOOE allocations) from the General Fund to the Special Trust Fund at year-end.
Section 5.1.1.3: Revert all LDRRMF balances remaining unused after five (5) years to the General Fund as unappropriated surplus.
Reclassify the amount of ₱4,754,792.09, currently recognized under the General Fund as Trust Liability – DRRMF, to the Special Trust Fund – DRRMF to reflect proper fund source and enhance monitoring.
Suggested Adjusting Entry:
In case at year-end there will be no proper cash transfer from GF to TF for the unexpended portion, the accounting office may use “Due to” and “Due from” to recognize the transfer:
Revert the amount of ₱2,984,547.12, representing unutilized LDRRMF balances from CYs 2018 and 2019, to the General Fund. This should be recorded as unappropriated equity to make the funds available for future appropriation for social services or other priority programs. (See illustrative journal entry number 7 in Annex C of COA Circular No. 2012-002)

We recommended the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Person to undertake the following measures:
Conduct a thorough evaluation of the proposed or attributed PPAs to ensure they are genuinely responsive to identified gender-related issues and concerns;
Provide additional supporting documentation or justification to substantiate the mainstreamed PPAs; and
Regularly update the GAD Database to facilitate the accurate identification, monitoring, and analysis of gender issues.
These actions will enhance the integrity and effectiveness of the municipality's GAD initiatives, ensure proper classification of PPAs, and align with relevant policies and guidelines.

We recommend that Management thoroughly review and assess all PPAs proposed under the 20% Development Fund to ensure their alignment with the intent and requirements of Section 287 of RA 7160 and the provisions of JMC 2020-001. Future appropriations should prioritize capital or investment projects that align with the LGU’s Local Development Plan and support the long-term development goals of the municipality.
We further recommend that management avoid charging operational, recurring, or welfare-type expenses (such as those intended for aid, supplies, and support programs) to the 20% DF and instead fund them through more suitable sources like the General Fund, SEF, LDRRMF, or other special accounts.

We reiterate our previous recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the following:
The Municipal Assessor, Municipal Accountant, and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile their respective records concerning the Land account. This may be achieved by conducting an annual physical inventory of properties and determining the acquisition cost of each land parcel included in the Land account.
In cases where a parcel of land was acquired through non-exchange transaction, such as donations, determine the prevailing fair market value at the date of acquisition;
The management to exhaust all possible means to document the legality of ownership of the lots through a Transfer Certificate of Title in the name of the City as required under Sec. 39 (2) of PD No. 1445 and Section 148 of COA Circular No. 92-386; and
Require the Municipal Assessor to request from the competent authority to appraise the properties without assigned costs so that these could be recorded properly in the books of accounts to fairly present the Land account in the financial statements.
Direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entries based on the results of the land inventory, using the acquisition cost as the basis of valuation. In cases where determining the original cost is impracticable, the fair market value at the time of valuation or assessed value (whichever is lower) may be used instead, as certified by the Municipal Assessor. Additionally, ensure that appropriate supporting documents such as TCTs and Tax Declarations are secured to substantiate the recorded land assets.

To strengthen internal control, boost payroll efficiency, and mitigate risks linked to manual cash disbursement, we reiterate our previous recommendations:
The Municipality should shift to an ATM-based payroll system for all employees, including regular, contractual, and job order personnel. This will streamline payroll disbursement, reduce cash handling risks, and improve efficiency.
Coordination with authorized banking institutions should be undertaken to facilitate payroll account enrollment for employees.
The Municipal Treasurer and Disbursing Officer should implement stricter internal control measures in compliance with Section 124 of PD 1445 to minimize risks associated with handling large cash advances.

We recommended the Management the following:
Procure a secured storage cabinet specifically designed for accountable forms;
Restrict access to accountable forms only to authorized personnel; and
Require personnel handling accountable forms to log each issuance of Accountable Forms for monitoring and accountability.

We recommended that:
The Municipal Accountant record the book reconciling items pertaining to unrecorded withdrawals totaling ₱5,247.57 in absolute value; and
The Municipal Accountant revert to Cash in Bank- LCCA account and recognize the liability for the equivalent amount of stale checks totaling ₱4,250.00.
Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant continue his effort in complying the preparation of Bank Reconciliation Statements for fair presentation of Cash in Bank accounts of the Municipality.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to:
for long outstanding and/or dormant receivables
Conduct a thorough aging analysis of all receivables to determine their collectability;
Resume collection activities, including issuing follow-up demand letters to concerned individuals and entities. Further, coordinate with the Legal Office for possible legal actions against delinquent accounts that remain unresponsive; and
Facilitate completion of the documents required to make request for write-off of the dormant accounts in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005 for fair presentation of the accounts.
for abnormal credit balances
Review all transactions under the Due from Officers and Employees account to determine the causes of the negative balances;
Cross-check with payroll records, remittances, and deduction summaries to ensure proper accounting treatment; and
Prepare and record necessary adjusting journal entries to correct erroneous postings and restore account balances to their correct nature.
for inadequate documentation of receivables
Ensure that subsidiary ledgers contain complete and detailed records of each receivable, including sufficient supporting documents to verify the accuracy and completeness of recorded balances, ensuring compliance with P.D. No. 1445.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant take up the necessary adjusting journal entries in the books of accounts to reflect the actual insurance expenses incurred and recognize the unexpired balances under the Prepaid Insurance and Other Prepayments accounts for CY 2024.
Henceforth, faithfully comply with the provisions of IPSAS regarding the application of accrual basis of accounting and strictly observed the proper use of accounts as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare a detailed lapsing schedule for all items of PPE which must show the accurate and comprehensive computation of depreciation using the appropriate guidelines and policies adopted by the Municipality and reflect all adjustments accordingly.
Thereafter, the Municipal Accountant regularly maintain such schedule to support the balances reported in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Record the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to update the balance of the accounts by reclassifying the affected items into their proper PPE category;
Review the estimated useful life assigned to the affected accounts and recalculate depreciation in case of change in estimate; and
Ensure that transactions are properly and consistently classified in accordance with the description of the accounts.

We, therefore, recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Record all on-going projects under the appropriate Construction in Progress account equal to the amount of percentage of completion vis-à-vis the contract cost;
Reclassify all completed projects from CIP to the appropriate PPE account upon completion and consequently compute depreciation from the date of completion; and
Coordinate with the Engineering Office to develop a tracking and reporting mechanism on the status of projects under constructions-in-progress and those that are completed to prevent delays in recognition of the same to their appropriate accounts even in the event of non-availability of actual billings from contractors.

To strengthen internal controls over the disbursement of fuel, we recommended that Management:
Enforce strict adherence to the provisions of COA Circular No. 77-61 to facilitate monitoring and preparation of reports relative to fuel consumption, thereby minimizing wastage and unnecessary expenditures. Specifically, instruct drivers to properly accomplish the Driver's Trip Tickets and adequately prepare the monthly report of official travels and monthly report of fuel consumption of each vehicle; and
Ensure that no private vehicles benefit from government-funded fuel allocations, hence require the GSO to issue a monthly certification, to support the monthly Statement of Account from the gasoline station, confirming that all vehicles receiving fuel are LGU-owned.

In line with the foregoing observations, we recommended that Management strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005. Consequently, we recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Conduct regular review and reconciliation with concerned NGAs/ LGUs Accountants and implement stricter monitoring on the status of the utilization of funds;
Ascertain that all recorded receivables are legitimate, properly documented, and supported by valid agreements or transactions. Any receivables found to be without sufficient basis, lacking documentation, or no longer collectible should be promptly adjusted or written off in accordance with applicable rules and regulations; and
Enforce liquidation by regularly sending demand letters to concerned NGAs and LGUs for the immediate submission on the fund utilization reports and/or liquidation reports.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive direct the following department heads/committee for compliance with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 25, 2015:
Municipal Accountant
Prepare the required copies of PPE Local Road Network Ledger Cards (Annex D format) per Item VII a.1 of COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 25, 2015; and
Update the yearly required disclosures as needed;
Municipal General Services Officer
Prepare and submit Local Road Inventory and Road Maps; and
Keep a complete Local Road Network Property Cards for all roads and its components (Annex E format) per Item VII b.2 of COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 25, 2015.
We also recommended that the Inventory Committee submit Report on the Physical Count of Local Road Network System (RPCLRN) supported with Local Road Network Maps (Annex C format) per Item VI.3 of COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 25, 2015.

We, therefore, recommended that the management ensure full compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2022-004 dated May 31, 2022 as supplemented by COA Circular No. 2024-006 dated March 14, 2024. Specifically, classify all purchased and/or issued semi-expendables to their appropriate accounts and prepare and maintain all applicable prescribed forms, reports, and registries.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Exert effort to determine the nature of the unremitted prior period balances and effect immediately their remittance. Thereafter, ensure the faithful, regular, and full remittances of mandatory deductions in compliance with the requirements and deadlines set by the respective agencies; and
Observe consistency in the disclosures presented in the Notes to Financial Statements in accordance with Paragraph 15 of Philippine Accounting Standards (PAS) No. 1.

We, therefore, recommended that the Municipal Treasurer immediately account for the Three Thousand Eighty-Four (3,084) pieces of missing accountable forms to establish the correct balances of accountability for said accountable forms. Otherwise, a Notice of Suspension will be issued due to the absence of forms reported as issued.
Likewise, we highly recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Treasurer to exercise the diligence of a good father of a family in monitoring the movement of the accountable forms and the collections and deposits of cash to strengthen the internal control of the agency in safeguarding its assets and to prevent the incurrence of loss or misuse of government funds and property.

We recommended that Management:
Submit legal bases or any exemption to the law and/or concurrence of COMELEC for further consideration in audit for the appointment of the Sectoral Representative for Agriculture; and
Explain why the payments of salaries, RATA, communication allowances, bonuses and other emoluments should not be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to:
Strictly comply with the regulation on the rendition of accounts and financial reports in order not to delay the verification and post-audit activities; and
Observe the due dates of submission of the required financial reports and documents to the Audit Team by setting timelines of submission from Treasury Office to Accounting Office. Likewise, maintain processing and payment of transactions with complete supporting documents. Without doing so, delays will affect both the Treasury Office and the Accounting Office on submission of accounts.

We, therefore, recommended that the:
MGSO regularly monitor the expiration and renewal of registration of all motor vehicles and apply or renew the registration of all serviceable motor vehicles of the Municipality with LTO and contracts of insurance with the GSIS to avoid additional expenses in terms of penalties and payment of damages to third party liabilities in case of vehicular accidents; and
MGSO and Municipal Accountant reconcile their records regarding the updated list of motor vehicles owned by the municipality.
Likewise, the management must pursue and prioritize the conduct of physical inventory count of all its PPE considering its necessity and importance.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), through its secretariat, submit all contracts, together with all the supporting documents forming part thereof, within five (5) days from its perfection to the Auditor to enable the latter to conduct auditorial review, before these contracts are forwarded to COA Regional Office for technical evaluation without prejudice to the penal clause cited under COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.
In addition, we also recommended that Management assess the workload of the personnel in-charge of the submission of contract documents, ensuring greater efficiency in the delivery of public services. This assessment should also help determine if there is a need for additional personnel to support this function.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the LDRRMO to continuously monitor the implementation of PPAs to facilitate the timely completion thereof;
Ensure that PPAs funded out of the DRRM funds are well-planned and meticulously reviewed by the LDRRMC; and
Instruct the LDRRMO to prepare the LDRRMFIP as prescribed in COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 for the current year. Additionally, in a separate section, includes the list of project and activities to be funded from the unexpended LDRRMF in previous years under the Special Trust Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer inform the contractor to carry out rectification of the defects to maintain the durability of the concrete pavement otherwise defects will become deficiency.

3. 	To provide proper disclosure on the disaggregate amount of the negative Due to BIR in the Notes to the Financial Statement in compliance with IPSAS 1.

observations.recommendation:

a) Treasury Office provide the required list of taxpayers to the Accounting
Office every start of the year; and
b) Accounting Office
i) set up RPT/SET Receivables based on the list of taxpayers furnished
by the Treasury Office;
ii) act on the issues contained in the CY 2021 audit report, including
the reconciliation of the RPT/SET Receivable and Deferred
RPT/SET accounts; and
iii) prepare and maintain complete SLs for all the receivable accounts.
2.15 The Accounting and Treasury Offices agreed to implement the audit
recommendations.

3.20 We reiterated our recommendation that the -
a) Inventory Committee:
i) continue to count the PPEs of the Municipality and improve its
preparation of RPCPPE and reconcile the same with the accounting
records; and
ii) tag all PPEs with inventory stickers to ease the count of items;
b) LCE instruct the -
a) GSO to prepare and maintain complete PCs and reconcile with the
accounting records; and
b) Accounting Office and the GSO to conduct the preliminary
activities to clean up the PPE accounts.
3.21 We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a) reconcile the PPELC with the GL balances; and
b) review the transactions and records mentioned in paragraphs 3.12 and
3.15, respectively, and effect adjusting journal entries if necessary.
3.22 The LCE committed to implement all the audit recommendations regarding PPE.

4.8 We reiterated our recommendation that the LCE enjoin -
a) the Property Officer/GSO, Municipal Accountant and the Municipal
Engineer to coordinate with each other and abide by the said Circular
in maintaining the pertinent reports and property records to be able to
provide reliable basis on the amounts presented in the FS;
c) the Municipal Accountant to present completely in the Notes to the FS
the total road network system.
4.9 We further recommended that the Inventory Committee to count all the local
roads and prepare the RPCLRN in accordance with the format prescribed by COA
Circular 2015-008.
4.10 The concerned offices agreed to comply with all the audit recommendations
concerning Local Road Network.

5.8 We reiterated our recommendations that the Accounting Office:
i) prepare and maintaining necessary SLs of the payable accounts and
disclose the unverified balances to decrease the unreliable amounts; and
ii) to exert extra effort in verifying the payable accounts' negative and
dormant balances and effect necessary adjustments in the book of accounts.

6.20 We recommended that the LCE instruct:
a) the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to
i) request and obtain a copy of the deed of loan assignment from
MDFO and LBP;
ii) thoroughly review the contract to ensure that all terms and
conditions are understood and agreed upon;
iii) reconcile both the accounting and treasury records with the actual
amount of loan obligation by the Municipality; and
iv) ensure that all relevant documents are obtained, verified and
properly recorded before any financial transactions are made;
b) the Municipal Accountant, the Municipal Treasurer and concerned
Officials of LBP Tarlac lending center to-
i) determine and provide evidence to prove that the imposed LBP
interest rates were based on market-reference rate; and
ii) consider to incorporate in the provisions of succeeding loan
contracts the requirements prescribed by law and jurisprudence
regarding interest rates.
6.21 The LCE agreed that all of the audit recommendations regarding the Loans Payable
account will be implemented.

7.28 On the other hand, we reiterated our recommendation that the LCE instruct the -
a) MPDO to prioritize PPAs so that specific dates or schedules of
implementation were indicated in AIP; and
b) the BAC, BAC Secretariat, and the Accounting Office to submit the
lacking documents as stated in paragraph 7.25 and an explanation and/or
justifications on the other audit observations.
7.29 We also recommended that the LCE instruct the BAC, BAC Secretariat, and
the Accounting Office to submit the lacking documents as stated in paragraph 7.25
and an explanation and/or justifications on the other audit observations.

8.41 We reiterated our recommendation that the LCE instruct the Local DRRM
Office (LDRRMO) to:
a) comply with COA Circular No. 2012-02 on the preparation of LDRRMFIP;
and
b) properly identify PPAs to be charged from the available LDRRMF to
maximize the utilization of the said fund;
8.42 We further recommended that for future similar transactions, the LCE instruct
the concerned officers to submit distribution lists completely indicating necessary
details to facilitate the conduct of validation.
8.43 We also recommended that the LCE instruct the:
a) LDRRMO to review implemented projects and ensure that only allowable
expenses resulting from disaster risk management activities are charged
from LDRRMF;
b) Municipal Treasurer to
i) make representation to the DILG, DBM and NDRRMC and inquire if
LGU San Manuel can legally transfer unutilized LDRRMF based on the
actual collections; and
ii) transfer the correct amount of unexpended LDRRMF to the TF from
the GF;
c) Municipal Accountant to
i) reconcile her records and effect adjusting entries when necessary; and
ii) be cautious in the preparation of accounting entries and reporting of
LDRRMF so as not to mislead stakeholders and cause inappropriate
decisions;
d) concerned officers to submit explanation or justification on noted
deficiencies stated in paragraph 8.34; and
e) Inspection Officer to explain why the procured rice in December 2023 is
already nearing spoilage in the same month, the reason why the said
commodity is distributed immediately.

9.10 We recommended that the Accounting Office
a) together with the Treasury Office,
i) to be cautious in the selection of accounts for the recognition of funds
received from other agencies so as not to misstate the financial
records;
ii) stop the practice of transferring Barangay shares from the RPT and
PNP shares from fines collected to the Trust Fund before their
distribution to minimize funds being monitored in the Trust Fund
books; and
iii) transfer to the PNP only the 20% share of the said agency from the
apprehension fines;
b) adjust the misstated accounts based on the audit observations presented in
paragraph 9.9; and
c) dedicate its utmost efforts towards completing the essential records. This
will not only streamline the monitoring of received funds but also enhance
the effectiveness of decision-making processes.
9.11 We further recommended that the LCE evaluate the personnel complement of
the Accounting Office and implement remedial actions if necessary.
9.12 Furthermore, we recommended that concerned Offices -
a) evaluate the purposes, related laws and rules, and agreements with the
source agencies regarding the funds received from the PCSO, PHIC and
PGT for their efficient and beneficial utilization; and
b) go over the agreement with the DSWD concerning the implementation of
the Supplemental Feeding Program, especially on the required
procurement mode, and comply with the same, so as to attain all the
objectives of the program.

10.19 We recommended that the LCE direct the -
a) BAC and its Secretariat, together with the Municipal Accountant, to
i. faithfully comply with the requirements of revised IRR of R.A. No.
9184 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012; and
ii. submit the lacking documents as enumerated in paragraph 10.11; and
b) concerned officials to comply with the posting/reporting requirements of
the succeeding DBM Memorandum on the Guidelines on the Release and
Utilization of the Shares of LGUs from the collection of Excise Tax.
10.20 The Officers of the Municipality concurred to implement all the audit
recommendations pertaining to the utilization of Tobacco Excise Tax.

11.11 We recommended that the Municipality consider the creation of MENRO so
that implementation of the programs contained in the SWMP, coordination with
other councils and boards, SWM budget preparation, request and monitoring, and
encouragement of community cooperation and participation will be undertaken
efficiently and effectively.

12.9 We recommended that the Treasury Office see to it that issuance of checks to
payees are done immediately by its personnel so that DVs will be forwarded to the
Accounting Office at once or considering the use of e-payments so as to streamline
the process
12.10 We also recommended that the Accounting Office monitor the submission of
DVs by the Treasury Office and instantly remind the latter for any unsubmitted DV.

13.13 We recommended that the GFPS ensure that a) GPBs are submitted on time;
b) PPAs in GPB are consistent with the AR for proper evaluation; and c) furnish the
Audit Team with the justification on the over expenditure on PWDs gadget with the
corresponding supplemental budget or re-alignment, if there is any.
13.14 Likewise, we reiterated the audit recommendations that the GFPS
a) request or scout for trainings on the HGDG and PIMME;
b) work on the passage of comprehensive guidelines on the educational
assistance program;
c) submit justification why the disbursements exceeded the CY 2022
budgeted amounts;
d) submit copies of re-alignment orders on the noted observations in
paragraph 16.14 of the CY 2022 audit report; and
e) render an explanation as to the noted discrepancies between the reported
utilization per CY 2022 GAD AR and utilization as actually computed.

14.8 We reiterated the audit recommendation that the LCE consider the further
improvement of the guidelines on communication expense, especially the control
over the cellphones paid out of the Municipality’s resources.

15.25 We reiterated the audit recommendations that the -
a) LSB
i) formulate the required three-year program so that utilization of the
SEF would be maximized through coordination with the
Municipal and Provincial investment and development plans; and
ii) submit justification on the repair of the district office in CY 2021
for the Audit Team’s evaluation;
b) Municipal Accountant be cautious in the preparation of reports to be
posted in conspicuous places; and
c) LSB and the Property Officer to coordinate with the DepEd about the
obtaining of a separate clearance from the LGU by DepEd personnel prior to
their retirement, resignation, transfer, or reassignment to strengthen
control over properties procured under the SEF.
15.26 Likewise, we recommend that the Local Chief Executive/Chairman of the LSB
instruct -
a) concerned offices/personalities to indicate specific PPAs in the
budget and the corresponding expense class (MOOE or Capital
Outlay) in the LSB budgets for proper monitoring and evaluation;
b) end users to prepare PR with complete specifications of the
requested items;
c) canvassers to request prospected suppliers’ representative to
indicate brands in the request for quotation forms;
d) PO/Inspection and Acceptance Reports preparers to indicate
brands and terms in the forms being prepared;
e) GSO/Property Officer to indicate the brands, serial numbers and
complete specifications of property/inventory in the ARE/ICS; and
f) BAC to consider requesting quotations from other suppliers to have
a more responsive bids.
15.27 We also recommended that the defective door knobs of the constructed 2-
classroom building be investigated so as to implement necessary actions. If it is due
to inferior quality of materials, the contractor should be held liable. On the one hand,
if it is due to the negligence of the end users, appropriate actions should also be
undertaken. PPAs implemented utilizing the SEF should also be closely monitored
so that the fund will be maximized for the attainment of its purpose.

16.14 We recommended that the LCE direct concerned offices to set up attainable
projected revenues to encourage goal attainment and better resource allocation.
16.15 Likewise, we recommended that the Treasury Office -i. examine the books of
more businesses applying for the renewal of their permits in accordance with the
provisions of the LGC and LTOM; ii. use other appropriate audit procedures to
properly assess the tax liabilities of the said establishments; and iii. properly file
documents of establishments issued with permits for easy verification.

17.7 We recommended that the Treasury Office study the purpose of the mayor’s
clearance and expedite the request for the adoption of an Ordinance for the
collection of same in addition to the business tax collected to establish the legal
framework required for collecting said fees.

18.9 However, we reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Mayor instruct the
i. HRMO and Accounting Office to exert more effort to lessen, if not to eliminate,
discrepancies of their records with the GSIS, HDMF, and PHIC; and
ii. Accounting Office to employ best efforts in the recording of transactions so that
the actual withheld amount for a month will be remitted the following month.

We recommended that Management refrain from the practice of paying and charging prior year expenses to current year appropriation for proper matching of income and expenditures to be able to reflect the correct balance of net income for a particular period. This is in adherence with the above-quoted provisions of R.A. No.7160 and PD 1445.

Expenses of the previous year paid on the current year should be recorded in the books with the following entry:

On the year expense was incurred

     Dr. Expense Account
                 Cr. Accounts Payable	


     On the year expense was paid

     Dr. 	Accounts Payable
      Cr.	Cash
observations.managementAction: At year-end, all expenses incurred during the last quarter were obligated and recorded as liabilities. This practice was implemented to avoid the previous method of charging expenses to the current budget.
observations.reason: Adjusting entries were made by the Accountant.

Adjust the depreciation to reflect the correct carrying amount of PPE in accordance with Item 42 of IPSAS No. 3.

Assigning a focal person (e.g. Municipal Administrator, Municipal Accountant) to annually consolidated all the needed data from various offices concerned and ensure completeness of details such as target date of implementation and reasons for partial/delay/implementation.
Directing the department heads of all concerned offices to promptly provide the required data to the focal person to avoid delays on submission of the AAPSI.

Furnish a copy of the AAPSI, either in printed form or by way of electronic documents to the following:

The Senate Committee on Finance
