observations.recommendation:
Recommendation:
We have recommended that the inventory committee immediately conduct and submit duly reconciled required report on physical count of all the property, plant and equipment of the municipality as prescribed under Section 124 of the NGAS, Volume I of the LGU to ascertain the accuracy and validity of the  presented  PPE amount in the financial statement.

Recommendation:
The Municipal Treasurer shall deposit intact all the collections as well as all collections turned over by the collectors/tellers with the authorized depository bank daily or not later than the next banking day.  We have also recommended the practice of due diligence in recording transactions to avoid abnormal balances in the financial statements.

Recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer to discontinue utilizing the Petty Cash Fund for payment of travelling expenses of Municipal officials and employees instead payment of such shall be made through check.

Recommendation:
We have recommended that regular monitoring of checks issued by the Municipality must be done to avoid stale checks. Moreover, adjusting journal entries should be prepared by the Municipal Accountant with respect to stale checks by making an entry as follows:
Debit:  Cash in Bank				xxx
Credit: Accounts Payable 			xxx
To revert to Cash in Bank checks issued which became stale
Likewise, a copy of the Journal Entry Voucher must be furnished to the Municipal Treasurer to effect proper adjustment in the cashbooks.

Recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant determine the details of the accounts Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds and make the necessary adjusting entries to reconcile the reciprocal account.

Recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant make the following necessary adjusting entries to present fairly the financial statements of the Municipality:
Dr.	Due from Officers and Employees			xx
Cr.	Accounts Receivable					xx

Recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant analyze and explain the nature of transaction that brought about the affected Liability accounts to abnormal balances and subsequently undertake corrective measures to come up with appropriate balances of the accounts. This is to ensure that the financial statements are fairly presented.

Recommendation:
In view of the foregoing deficiencies, we have recommended to Management the following:
To reconcile the records pertaining to the unremitted contributions and loan repayments and to effect immediately the remittance of long overdue accounts, if any;
2.	To remit intact all the GSIS contributions and loan amortizations on or before the 10th day of the following month to ensure that officials and employees enjoy the GSIS benefits to the maximum and as well as to avoid incurrence of penalties.

Recommendation:
We have recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to remit all PAG-IBIG contributions especially those pertaining to prior years to avoid delay in the processing of members'/employees' claims and prevent possible forfeiture of the benefits due them.
Likewise, we have recommended regular review/analysis of the details of the subsidiary ledger balances of Due to Pag-IBIG account to determine the reason for the discrepancies and effect the necessary adjustment/s thereof.

Recommendation:
We have recommended therefore that Management institute the following control measures:
Regular reconciliation of records pertaining to the under and over remittance of premium contributions;
Maintenance of employees' individual ledger cards to monitor the deductions of each employee; and
PhilHealth premium contributions deducted from employees must be remitted within the prescribed period.

Recommendation:
With the above observations, we have recommended that:
1.	The Accounting Office transfer all unexpended/unobligated balance of the Quick Response Fund and DRRM-MOOE to the Special Trust Fund under the account “Trust Liability-DRRM Fund” in the Trust Fund books.
2.	The Treasury Office undertake actual transfer of funds pertaining to the unexpended/unobligated balance of the Quick Response Fund and DRRM-MOOE from General Fund depository bank account to the Trust Fund depository bank account.
3.	Strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002 prescribing accounting and reporting guidelines for Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund must be observed.

Recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant, HRMO and Municipal Treasurer ensure compliance with the requirements set forth under Section 52 of General Appropriation Act (GAA) of 2019 and adopt control measures to ensure that employee’s monthly net take home pay shall not be lower than Five Thousand Pesos (P5,000.00) after deducting mandatory deductions and other obligations.

Recommendation:
Recommendation:
We have recommended that:
Management create Inventory Committee responsible in the conduct of physical count of inventories in line with the provisions of Section 124 of NGAS Manual for Local Government Units (LGUs), Volume I which states that the Local Chief Executive shall require periodic physical inventory of supplies and properties to be conducted every semester and to be reported in the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) which then shall be submitted to the Auditor concerned not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively.
Justify the manner of derecognition made in the Inventory account pertaining to the non-moving inventories amounting to P566,038.00.
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to effect the necessary adjustment in the books of accounts to bring the balances of the said inventory accounts to correct balances.

Recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer exert utmost effort to meet the demands of timely reporting and submission of accounts.  This is to avoid undue delays in the conduct of post audit and preparing the required reports in compliance with existing laws, rules and regulations.
Management’s comment/action:
Accounts of the LGU for the first quarter of the ensuing year were submitted within the reglementary period attributed to the stringent management of the Local Chief Executive.

Recommendation:
We have recommended that the concerned officials to exert utmost efforts to meet the demands of timely reporting and submission of accounts. This is to avoid undue delay precluding the team from timely conduct of audit and rendering of the required reports in compliance with the existing laws, rules and regulations. Reports when submitted on time are useful information that can aid management in decision making.

Recommendation:
In view of the above-mentioned observations, we have recommended the following:
That Management direct the Municipal Accountant and LDRRM Officer to submit a copy of Report on the Sources and Utilization of the DRRMF using the format provided in Annex B of COA Circular No.2012-002 to facilitate the review and monitoring of the sources and utilization of the fund.
2.	We have also recommended that the concerned officials to ensure the regular and timely submission of the said report in compliance with the above-mentioned COA Circular.
Management’s Comment/Action:
The LDRRM Officer has already submitted their report for the first quarter but consistent submission within the reglementary period is yet to be observed.

We have recommended that:
The Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to submit the lacking documents to facilitate the contract review and analysis of the auditors.
During bid opening, the BAC should take a thorough review of the qualifications of a bidder if there is reasonable ground to believe that a misrepresentation has been made or there have been changes in the bidder's capability to undertake the project or legal documents presented were expired at the time of bid.
The Municipal Engineer to reassess/review pertinent provisions, standards and guidelines in the preparation of detailed quantity and cost estimates for a uniform application of tax rates and accurate valuation of Approved Budget for the Contract.
4. The BAC strictly comply with the provisions of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 in furtherance to the principles of transparency and competitiveness.

Recommendation:
We restate our previous year’s recommendation that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) ensure the submission of the contracts, P.O.s and its supporting documents within five (5) working days from the issuance or execution pursuant to the COA Circular No. 2009-001 in order for the audit team to conduct timely review of the documents submitted and to enforce immediate compliance of the deficiencies, if any.
Management’s Comment/Action:
The Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) was able to submit Bid documents on the seven (7) projects out of the twenty-nine projects presented in the above table upon receipt of the AOM. Contract review is yet to be undertaken by the team to determine completeness and correctness of the submitted documents.

Recommendation:
After all these audit findings discovered from the paid disbursement vouchers, we have recommended the following:
That Management shall regularly check and verify the purpose of the requisition in the Purchase Request in order to facilitate review and accountability.
Management avoid reimbursement as a mode of procurement and adopt public bidding or other alternative modes of procurement provided under RA 9184, in order to promote transparency and to avoid possible source of irregularities.
Regular purchases by the Municipality should be made through check disbursement payable directly to the supplier/creditor.
Strict compliance with BIR regulations should be adhered to by the Municipal Accounting Office on withholding of taxes to avoid possible tax penalties which might be incurred by the Municipality.
5.	Lastly, we have recommended that concerned official ensure that all disbursements are supported with complete documentation and signature of proper officials including suppliers before processing/approving the claims.

Recommendation:
In view of the foregoing observations, we have recommended the following:
Require the concerned officials to secure and submit documents such as distribution list or acknowledgement receipt as proof of receipt by the end-user to support the disbursement.
2.	Management should refrain from paying purchases thru reimbursement in compliance with established rules on disbursements. This would ensure that proper taxes shall be deducted and remitted to the Bureau of Internal Revenue.
Management’s Comment/Action:
There was partial submission of Acknowledgement Receipt from recipients on the foregoing deliveries. Consistent payment of purchases directly to the supplier is yet to be observed.

Recommendation:
We have recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) see to it that the full descriptions and/or specifications of the goods to be procured should properly be indicated in the procurement documents and other relevant supporting documents in compliance with the Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 9184.
Furthermore, we require submission of above-listed documentary deficiencies and specific description of items procured on the above transaction.
Management’s Comment/Action:
The Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) submitted the corresponding Bid documents upon receipt of the AOM. Contract review is yet to be undertaken by the team to determine completeness and correctness of the submitted documents.

Recommendation:
We have recommended for the Municipal Budget Officer to diligently and regularly monitor Special Education Fund and adopt budgetary control measures to avoid incurrence of deficit.
We have also recommended for the Municipal Accountant to analyze the significant increase of the Depreciation Expense Account and effect the necessary adjustments, if any.

Recommendation:
We have recommended the agency to continue to abide with the guidelines on the appropriation and utilization of the 20% development fund as mentioned in the DILG and DBM Joint Circular 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011.

We have recommended for Management to institute the following control measures:
To instruct the concerned officials to analyze the account Due to BIR and determine cause of the abnormal balance;
To devise or institute internal control in processing tax filing and remittances to ensure that the taxes withheld are remitted on time.
To take corrective measures to protect the interest of the Municipality.
4.	Remit intact all withheld taxes to the BIR to avoid paying penalties, surcharges and interest arising from violations. Failure to do so may result to imposition of sanction imposed by the BIR.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review thoroughly the Financial Statements especially the Notes to Financial Statements to comply with the PPSAS and COA Circulars relative to the preparation and presentation of the FS by (1) providing adequate disclosures in the Notes to Financial Statements; and (2) presenting the breakdown, details and figures of all other accounts being cross-referenced in the Financial Statements.
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that Management require the concerned personnel to conduct periodic physical inventory count of supplies and submit Report of Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semester, respectively. Likewise, write down of non-moving inventories will be in accordance with prescribed rules and regulations.
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare and maintain the supporting schedules of the Accounts Receivable for all funds to ensure the verifiability of the account and  the accuracy of the amounts presented.
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the following:
the Municipal Accountant to:
Ensure that the officers and employees with outstanding or unliquidated cash advances should not be granted additional cash advances, until the previous ones are settled, to preclude accumulation of unliquidated cash advances;
Ensure that all regulations pertaining to cash advances should be strictly enforced;
Monitor the outstanding cash advances of officials and employees and demand immediate settlement after its purpose has been served; and
Issue demand letter to settle the unliquidated cash advances.
the Municipal Treasurer to:
If warranted, withhold the salaries of those who persistently fail or refuse to comply despite repeated demand for liquidation and/or file appropriate administrative and criminal charges against them to compel liquidation.
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that Management, whenever the need arises to grant cash advances to officers and employees other than those existing Disbursing Officers, ensure that the authority issued by the Head of the Agency is availed of and documented, along with the certification from the Municipal Accountant that previous cash advances have been liquidated and accounted for in the books, and the duly approved application for fidelity bond adequately covering the total cash advances granted.
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that Management set aside the grant of incentives in the form of honoraria/subsidy to national government employees until the Municipality has fully implemented the Modified Salary Standardization Schedule and other conditions prescribed in DBM Compensation Policy Guidelines 98-1.
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

We, therefore recommended that Management through the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office to:
Monitor the utilization of LDRRMF focusing on programs, projects and activities (PPAs) requiring the highest priority in terms of urgency and/or impact on four (4) thematic areas of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management;
Ensure efficient and effective implementation of all programmed projects identified during the budget year to optimize the utilization of the fund and to warrant that goals and objectives of reducing disaster risk and enhancing disaster preparedness and response capabilities be achieved; and
Direct the MDRRM Officer to submit monthly the Monthly Report on Utilization of LDRRM Fund to COA and DILG.
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

We, therefore recommended that Management through the Municipal Treasurer to send demand letters to delinquent taxpayers and conduct an information campaign for stricter tax collection measures to all real property owners within the Municipality with emphasis of granting discounts when paid before due dates and remind taxpayers of remedies that can be exercised by the Municipality in case of delinquencies with the possibility of losing their property for not paying at all.
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Management through the Municipal Accountant require the Implementing Agencies (Schools and Barangays) for the immediate liquidation of the reported balances.  Likewise, ensure that subsequent grants of financial assistance for fund transfers have an attached certification from the Municipal Accountant indicating that the funds previously transferred to the Implementing Agency (IA) have been liquidated and accounted for in the books in compliance with the documentary requirements under COA Circular No. 2012 before any funds be released.  After which, demand submission of liquidation reports from the recipient party as indicated in the Memorandum of Agreement before taking up adjustment in the books.
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Management conduct an investigation on the status of these Other Receivables to various individuals and discuss with the appropriate committee in the Sangguniang Bayan any remedial actions that will be undertaken to the advantage of the Municipality.  Moreover, please be guided with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-05 dated December 19, 2016 for the write-off of dormant receivables.
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that Management, thru the Municipal Accountant, submit certified copies of disbursement vouchers and supporting documents showing that corresponding liquidated damages were already deducted from the contractors’ claims on the above projects, if any.  Further, the Municipal Engineer in coordination with the MPDC to (a) ensure that the contractors engage catch-up program in order to fast track the completion of the projects with noted delays to avoid rescindment of contract as stipulated in Section 68 of RA 9184; (b) conduct an inventory of work accomplished by the contractor and impose liquidated damages of at least equal to one-tenth of one percent (0.1%) of the cost of the unperformed portion for every day of delay; and (c) intensify monitoring of project in order to avoid recurrence of delay to other projects by submission of Statement of Time Elapsed and Work Accomplished for all infrastructure projects.
The Audit Team shall continuously monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Budget Officer to prepare the Statement of Appropriations, Allotments, Obligations and Balances (SAAOB) and to submit the same to the audit team thru the Municipal Accountant.
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) continue to withhold and remit the mandatory PHIC contributions and the Municipal Accountant to conduct review or reconciliation for the noted difference of P164,536.41 in order for services to be availed of by the employees from Philhealth will not be delayed or affected.
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

7.5	We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to maintain special accounts in the general fund and prepare Financial Statements for its economic enterprises in order to have a more accurate basis in managing the operations of the economic enterprises of the Municipality.

8.8	We recommended and the Management agreed to stop the practice of charging inappropriate/improper expenditures against the 20% Development Fund and strictly adhere to the provisions of the abovementioned regulation.

12.7	We recommended and the Management agreed to:

No. 9003 by (i) including in the constituted MSWMB other members prescribed by the law and incorporating solid waste management PPAs in the 10-Yeas Solid Waste Management Plan and Annual Budget; and (ii) incorporating in the 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan provisions for funding, incentive programs, and privatization of solid waste management projects, if such is applicable and favorable to the agency;
(b) set aside enough budget for tipping fees and other related solid waste management PPAs to avoid over-spending; and

13.13	While we took cognizance of the relentless efforts of the LGU in supporting public basic education, the Audit Team recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE), in coordination with the members of the LSB, agreed to require the latter to follow and perform the activities in the preparation and approval of the SEF Budget with indicative timelines as prescribed by DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1, s. 2017 dated January 19, 2017 or the Revised Guidelines on the Use of SEF.  Moreover, the Chairman of the Local School Board and its members should establish effective control measures to monitor balances of appropriations in all periods in order to stop the incurrence of expenditures in which the budget was already exhausted and avoid incurring expenditures which are not allowed under the rules.

14.9	We recommended and the Management agreed to:
(a) strictly comply with the regulation on the rendition of accounts and submission of financial reports so that opportune verification and post-audit activities will be facilitated; and
(b) keenly observe the deadline of submission of the required financial reports and documents to the Audit Team.
15.5	We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to continue their rigid abidance to the aforementioned revenue regulation and laws and prompt remittance of the taxes due to the BIR.

16.6	We recommended and the Municipality agreed to continuously adhere to R.A. No. 8291, R.A. No. 9679 and the National Health Insurance Act of 2013 on the withholding of GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth contributions and loan amortizations from salaries of personnel and on remittance thereof within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the Management refrain from granting financial assistance to schools within the Municipality chargeable against its 20% development fund.

In cases where the Municipality grants assistance from general fund proper or Special Education Fund, establish controls on the grant of financial assistance to the schools by requiring the Municipal Accountant to initially recognize Due from National Government Agencies instead of having it expensed outright to maintain accountability of the funds and ensure that all financial assistance granted to various schools shall be supported with official receipts  Prior to granting of another FA, ensure that the funds are utilized in accordance with its intended purpose by requiring them to submit a duly certified-stamped received Statement of Works Accomplished (SWA) and Statement of Expenditures (SOE) with supporting documents.

We recommended that the Municipality fully observe the provisions of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017 on the use of 20% Development Fund, in order that proper utilization and implementation of the developmental projects be carried out effectively and efficiently. Ensure that the utilization of the 20% Development Fund be limited to programs, projects and activities that will contribute to the attainment of socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes and shall partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures and yield greater benefit to the people.

We recommended that Management revisit the grant of financial assistance to other LGUs especially to those belonging to higher income classification considering the financial capacity of the LGU.  If warranted, Management may also create proper representation with other LGUs in the Province of Isabela for the evaluation of the practice in exchanging financial assistance between and among the LGUs in the province.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reclassify the amounts granted under Donations-Barangays account to Due from Local Government Units and demand liquidation reports to ascertain if disbursements were made in accordance with the purpose.  Likewise, ensure that for subsequent fund transfers to be granted by the Municipality to the barangays, the documentary requirements under COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 such as the Memorandum of Agreement has to be complied with before any funds shall be released.  After which, demand submission of liquidation reports from the recipient party as indicated in the Memorandum of Agreement before taking up adjustment in the books.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant thoroughly review the disbursements pertaining to financial assistance/donations and ensure that these transactions are supported with complete documentary requirements. All grants must always be supported by request/solicitation letters and certified true copies of a valid Identification Card to determine the beneficiaries, together with other documentary evidences necessary for each type of assistance to establish the necessity of its granting in compliance with Section 4(6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445.

We likewise recommended that the Sangguniang Bayan members discontinue extending financial assistance regardless of the source of fund.  The practice of extending financial assistance is contrary to their mandated functions, duties and functions under Section 447 of RA 7160 as a legislative body, for the approval of the financial operations of the Municipality rests with the Local Chief Executive (LCE).  Instead, all letter-requests for financial assistance are recommended to be forwarded to the LCE with a marginal note of the SB concerned of “For approval” of the LCE.

We recommended that the Municipal Local School Board allocate the SEF budget only for eligible and priority programs, projects and activities, expressly stated in the DEPED-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1, s. 2017. Likewise, the total amount of P72,392.00 that was improperly charged against SEF be transferred to General Fund in order to adhere with the provision of Section 272 of RA 7160, otherwise disallowed in audit as this is considered an irregular expenditure.

We recommended that Management submit to the Audit Team the complete projects documents of (1) Construction of Multi-Purpose Building Phase 2; (2) Concreting of Barangay Cabaritan-Eden Road; (3) Construction of Gender and Development (GAD) Building Phase 1 immediately.  Likewise, ensure thereafter the submission of all contracts, purchase orders and the like together with the required supporting documents to facilitate auditorial review, evaluation and inspection in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-001.

We recommended that the Head of the Procuring Entity (HOPE) require all winning bidders of goods, infrastructure projects and consulting services to submit performance securities within the period as stipulated in the Notices of Award and prior to the signing of the contract in adherence to Section 39.1 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.  Otherwise, consider the forfeiture of their bid securities as provided for in Section 40.1 of the same IRR and to enforce Bid Securing Declaration by imposing penalties as provided for in GPPB Resolution No. 15-2014 dated June 20, 2014, if warranted.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant provide the Audit Team documents to prove that the P43,920.00 was indeed deducted from the employees’ earnings and a corresponding official receipt from the insurance company.

Likewise, the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer submit legal bases for the grant of government share for its employees’ private life insurance to justify that the total amount of P87,840.00 will not be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer provide the Audit Team with legal bases of charging the said monthly allowances granted to the BIR and the DAR personnel, the COMELEC Officer, the Day Care Workers stationed or assigned to the Municipality under the Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses in LGU budgets, as required by Section 4.1.2 of the Local Budget Circular No. 98 dated October 14, 2011, DBM Compensation Policy Guidelines No. 98-1 dated March 23, 1998, as supported by COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.

Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant fully support all the transactions made on this nature such as Office Order/s showing designations of said personnel, daily time records and accomplishments for our review, verification and appropriate action.

We recommended that the Management enforce all payments for the procurement of goods and services to withhold taxes due to the government pursuant to Revenue Regulation No. 2-98 issued May 17, 1998. As one of the withholding agents, the appropriate withholding taxes should be computed and reflected in the face of the voucher and the corresponding Certificate of Withholding Tax Form be issued to the concerned suppliers.

We recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and the Municipal Engineer, in coordination of the General Services Officer, submit to the Audit Team proofs of posting on the programs/projects undertaken in CY 2018, if any.  Likewise, ensure full compliance on the provisions of COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013 relative to the information and publicity of the Programs/Projects/Activities implemented by the Municipality, their monthly updates and the display of in conspicuous places within the location of the project regardless of nature, irrespective signboards of amount.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant affix her signature/certification on the computation of the HRMO of the terminal leave benefits of officials and employees to signify that computation was properly reviewed by the Accounting Unit as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant implement the monthly net take home pay for each employee pursuant to Section 48 of General Provisions of the General Appropriations Act (GAA) for FY 2018 also known as Republic Act No. 10964 and comply for the succeeding GAA issuances to maintain the required net take home pay as it changes from time to time.

Likewise, we recommended that the Authorized Approving Officer (AAO) for various loans coordinate with the Municipal Accountant before endorsing loans to consider the loan applicant’s Certificate of Net Pay duly certified by the Municipal Accountant in order to avoid instances wherein pay slips are modified in order to make his/her net pay appear to have increased to accommodate additional loan deductions in cases of applying of a specific loan.

Abad to refund the irregular claim of RATA otherwise Notice of Disallowance shall be issued.

We recommended that the Management shall ensure that the Auditor is furnished with the copy of the contracts within five (5) working days from its execution and shall be supported with complete documents as required in COA Circular 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the concerned department heads to submit the duly accomplished AAPSI on each findings and recommendations which were discussed in detail in Part II – Detailed Findings and Recommendations portion of the CY 2016 Annual Audit Report, in compliance with Section 96 of the General Provisions of RA No. 10717, otherwise known as the General Appropriations Act of 2016.

We recommended that the Management strictly adhere with the provisions of Sections 318, 319 and 320 of RA No. 7160 Local Government Code in the preparation and submission of the local budget. We further recommend that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Budget Officer to prepare the SAAOB as well as the Municipal Accountant to submit the Subsidiary Schedule of General Ledger Accounts in compliance with COA Circular 2010-01 dated March 2, 2010.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer take remedial measures/action on all obsolete/spoiled accountable forms pursuant to Section 99 of the Government Accounting and Auditing Manual, Volume I and request for a certificate of destruction from the concerned COA Auditor in order to properly drop the destroyed accountable forms from the Monthly Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, the Municipal Treasurer, and the Municipal Social Welfare Development Officer submit the lacking supporting documents as elaborated in items 1 to 2 on the liquidation of Social Pension to beneficiaries amounting to ₱1,914,500.00 without prejudice to the issuance of Notice of Suspension (NS).  Likewise, ensure completeness of supporting documents in all payments of Social Pension as emphasized in items 3 and 4 above in compliance with Section 4(6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445 otherwise known as the Government Auditing Code of the Philippines.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to do the following:
For Annex A and D - Status of Unliquidated Cash Advance and Status of Other Receivables, respectively:
Avail all remedies in the identification of the Accountable Officers and demand immediate settlement of all unliquidated cash advances from officials and employees concerned following the above cited regulations.
If warranted, withhold the salaries of those who persistently fail or refuse to comply despite repeated demand for liquidation and/or file appropriate administrative and criminal charges against them to compel liquidation.
Ensure that the officers and employees with outstanding or unliquidated cash advances should not be granted additional cash advances, until the previous ones are settled, to preclude accumulation of unliquidated cash advances.
Ensure that all regulations pertaining to cash advances should be strictly enforced.
Monitor the outstanding cash advances of officials and employees and demand immediate settlement after its purpose has been served.
For Annex B-Status of Fund Transfers to Other Government/Implementing Agencies (IAs), exhaust all remedies to liquidate the fund transfer made to the Barangay under Due from Local Government Agencies of which the amount of ₱6,789,000.00 was granted. Further make a reconciliation of the reported balance in Annex B and that of the Statement of Financial Position to reflect the correct balance of Due from Local Government Agencies account.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant do the following:
Make a detailed review of the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts and determine the cause of the unreconciled amounts.
Make the necessary adjustments/corrections in the books of accounts to present the correct balances of the affected accounts.
Exercise due care in recording and classifying the Municipality’s financial transactions to ensure that the balances of reciprocal accounts are at all times be the same. Likewise, submit a reconciliation statement showing the two accounts arrived at the same balances.

We recommended that the Management do the following:
Require the Inventory Committee to conduct an inventory of local roads under their jurisdiction and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of the Local Road Network at the end of every accounting period.
Pursue complete reconciliation of the RPCLRN and the updated Registry of Public Infrastructures in coordination with the General Services Officer and the Accountant through detailed review of prior year’s transactions in order to identify any unrecorded road networks.
Direct the Municipal Accountant to:
(a) Maintain Local Road Network Ledger Card and maintain separate subsidiary ledgers for the road and its components namely: road lot, road pavement, drainage and slope protection structures, and other miscellaneous structures;
(b) Support the beginning balance of Road Networks and its components amounting to ₱45,217,052.84 in the GL and Ledger Cards with working papers;
(c) Make details of the recorded road, highways and bridges which were closed to government equity in the prior years and subsequently support the recording in its books of accounts the amount of road works equivalent to the required transition provision for CY 2017, and
(d) Make disclose in the Notes to the Financial Statements as illustrated in Annex B of said circular.
Instruct the General Services Officer to (a) complete Local Road Inventory records with Road Map and Local Road Network Property Cards; and (b) submit the Report on Local Road Network of the LGU prepared by the Inventory Committee as shown in Annex A of the circular.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Inventory Committee to fast track the comprehensive physical count of all properties of the Municipality and submit the Report of the Physical Inventory of PPE to Commission on Audit pursuant to Sec. 124 of the NGAs Manual to establish the correct value of its assets.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly observe the proper account classification in recording transactions with reference to the prescribed Revised Chart of Accounts of LGUs of COA Circular 2015-009 dated December 21, 2015 for fair presentation in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Petty Cash Custodian to strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 48, Volume I of the Manual on the NGAS in the year-end closure of the petty cash fund.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) direct the Inventory Committee of the Municipality to conduct a complete physical count of PPE and label all properties/equipment showing complete description for easy identification and monitoring; and (b) require the designated Property Custodian and the Municipal Accountant to maintain complete and updated property cards and ledger cards, respectively, for reconciliation and control purposes.
We recommended that management stop the practice of charging inappropriate/improper expenditures to the 20% Development Fund and strictly adhere to the provisions of the abovementioned regulations.

We recommended that management allocate the mandatory minimum appropriation or funding for LCPC and instruct the council members to (a) ensure the proper implementation of the formulated PPAs in accordance with the above law and regulations;  and (b) consider outsourcing or partnership with private organizations and entities that provide sponsorship or financial assistance for the implementation of LCPC PPAs.

We recommended that the Local School Board coordinate with the Property Offices of both the LGU and the DepEd in order to strengthen the controls on property accountability and to accurately monitor the custodianship of government property.

We recommended that management, headed by the Municipal Mayor, (a) provide the Audit Team of the lacking documents; (b) instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the transactions are supported with complete documentation before certifying and effecting payment in order to avoid suspension and/or disallowance of the transactions; and (c) observe stringent compliance with the provisions of P.D. No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 on documentary requirements of transactions.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) (a) sign appropriate issuances for the GAD Focal Point System for more responsive planning; (b) instruct the GAD Executive Committee and Technical Working Group to facilitate the establishment of the GAD Database to aid in gender analysis and provide baseline data on the status of women and men in the LGU; (c) direct the GAD Focal Person to assess the extent of compliance through a compliance checklist of the elements of the institutional mechanisms in place and intensify efforts on those areas that should be in place; and (d) spearhead the creation of the GAD Code to support gender equality and gender-responsive governance.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Local Chief Executive (a) coordinate the efforts of the public and private sectors and implement the environmental laws on preserving and protecting the environment required under R.A. No. 9003; (b) maximize the use of the Materials Recovery Facility; (c) enforce the strict implementation of “No Littering” Ordinance; and (d) encourage the full participation of the barangays in the implementation of the R.A. No. 9003.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC to strictly comply with the requirements on the submission to COA of advance copy of contracts/purchase orders together with its supporting documents within five days from its perfection/approval.

We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendations that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to (a) strictly comply with the regulation on the rendition of accounts and financial reports in order not to delay the post-audit activities and communication of the results thereof; and (b) observe the prescribed period on the submission of the required financial reports and documents to the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Engineer to strictly comply with all the provisions of the aforementioned COA Circular in order to enhance transparency on its operations and monitoring of its PPAs.
We recommended that the Municipality continuously adhere to R.A. No. 8291. R.A. No. 9679 and the National Health Insurance Act of 2013.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommendedto the management the following:
Conduct the required annual physical count of the PPE as of December of every year. The actual count may be undertaken any month during the current year, but has to be updated at year-end. This procedure is a must in order to know the presence/physical existence of the fixed asset and their conditions; and to identify, segregate and reclassify unserviceable assets. Reconciliation of the result of the count with the accounting records is to be undertaken to effect the necessary adjustments; and to determine responsibility/accountability/liability for missing PPEs.
Maintain/update the subsidiary ledgers, PPE ledger cards, PARs and placing of property tags which are required under MNGAS for proper control and monitoring as well as for facility in identifying the subject PPEs.
For control and monitoring, make use of ICS for semi-expendable items to avoid loss, as these are usually small items.

We have recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) through the BAC Secretariat ensure the submission of the contracts, P.O.s and its supporting documents within five (5) working days from the issuance or execution pursuant to the COA Circular No.2009-001 in order for the audit team to conduct timely review of the documents submitted and to enforce immediate compliance of the deficiencies, if any.

We have recommended that Management refrain from the practice of paying and charging prior year expenses to current year appropriation for proper matching of income and expenditures to be able to reflect the correct balance of net income for a particular period. This is in adherence with the above-quoted provisions of R.A. No.7160 and PD 1445.
Expenses of the previous year paid on the current year should be recorded in the books with the following entry:
On the year expense was incurred
Dr. 	Expense Account		xxx
Cr.	Accounts Payable		xxx
On the year expense was paid
Dr. 	Accounts Payable		xxx
Cr.	Cash			xxx

We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer, being the Petty Cash Custodian strictly observe the limitation on payment from petty cash fund set forth by COA Circular No. 2012-001 and replenish the same on time in order to have enough fund to pay for recurring petty operating expenses of the Municipality.

We have recommended that management strictly comply with the General  Insurance Law as amended COA Circular No. 92-390 dated November 17, 1992 by conducting an annual inventory of all the insurable properties of the LGU. Subsequently prepare and submit a report verified by COA to the GSIS on or before October 31 of the ensuing year.
We have further recommended that adequate budget for payment of the required insurance premiums be provided and paid to the GSIS.

We have recommended that the designated paymaster first secure an approved payroll or list of payees indicating their net payments before a cash advance for salaries, wages and allowances be made to avoid balances at every month end that may result to misuse, misapplication or misappropriation of these payroll balances.
Also, the paymaster should refund to the Cashier/Collecting officer any unexpended payroll balances at year-end to comply with the regulation that all cash advances shall be liquidated at the end of the year.

We have reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that certification showing the number of workdays of actual service rendered in a month of an official/ department heads claiming RATA be submitted to serve as basis in the computation of actual claims. The certification should be issued by the Office of HRMO for department heads covered under Section 7.1 of LBC no. 103 and by the Sangguniang Bayan Secretary for those officials covered under Section 7.5 of the same LBC.
Likewise, we have recommended that Management discontinue paying claims for RATA in advance or until the purpose of the RATA has been served in compliance with the above-cited provisions of DBM.

We have recommended that Management should refrain from the following granting the following:
Mid-year Bonus and Cash Gift to consultants pursuant to Section 4 of Budget Circular No.2016-4.
Clothing allowance to consultants pursuant to Section 4 of Budget Circular No.2012-1.

We have reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the BAC Secretariat submit copies of Notice of Award duly received by the lowest complying responsive bidder as evidence for the successfully completed project to support the claim for honoraria.
We have further recommended that payroll for BAC honoraria be supported with complete documentary requirements in compliance with the above-cited COA Circular and to avoid suspension of the transaction in audit.

We have recommended the Local School Board to utilize the Special Education Fund for programs and projects defined under Section 272 of RA 7160 and the implementing guidelines on the utilization of SEF as set under DECS-DBM-DILG Joint Circulars No.01, 01-A and 01-B, series of 1998, 2000 and 2001 to avoid inappropriate utilization thereof and to maximize the benefit that could be availed under the fund.

We have recommended that the Management avoid reimbursement as a mode of procurement and adopt public bidding or other alternative modes of procurement provided under RA 9184, in order to promote transparency and to avoid possible source of irregularities. Furthermore, we also recommend that payment on regular purchase of various materials, supplies and equipment by the Municipality should be made through check in the name of the supplier/creditor. Likewise, compliance with BIR regulations should be adhered on withholding of taxes.

We have recommended that Management to consider only programs and projects covered under Section 3.0 of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No.2011-1 dated April 13, 2011 in the utilization of 20% development fund. We also recommend that funds allocated for non-related expenditures should be properly charge to their appropriate fund.

We have recommended that the Management require the concerned officials to exert utmost efforts to meet the demands of timely reporting and submission of accounts. This is to avoid undue delay precluding the team from timely conduct of audit and rendering of the required reports in compliance with the existing laws, rules and regulations. Reports when submitted on time are useful information that can aid management in decision making.

We have recommendedthat the Municipal Accountant determine the details of the accounts Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds and make the necessary adjusting entry/ies to reconcile to its reciprocal account in order to conform with sound internal control policies.

We have recommended for the management to exert more effort to collect these real property tax delinquencies especially those that have been long overdue for the municipality to have an additional income that could be used to fund various projects and programs that would be beneficial to its constituents.

We have recommended that utilization of GAD Fund be maximized and that proper planning be made so that all GAD related projects, programs and activities during the year be implemented immediately for the full attainment of its benefits beneficial to their constituents.
We have recommended submission of GAD Accomplishment Report within the timeframe and in the form prescribed by the Joint Circular for an efficient and effective assessment of implemented GAD PPAs.

     In this regard, the lacking documents  should  be  submitted  to
      ascertain the validity, legality and propriety of  the  FMR  projects.
      Otherwise,  the  corresponding  disbursements  may  be  suspended   or
      disallowed in audit.

We recommended that management ensure that all  its  procurement
      activities adhere with the prescribed rules and  procedures  and  that
      the same are properly supported with documents to prove  the  validity
      of the corresponding disbursements. Accordingly:


      • As regards the procurement of meals and lodging, management  submit
        an explanation why the canvass was made prior to the declaration of
        two failed biddings  and  the  approval  of  the  adoption  of  the
        alternative mode of negotiated procurement.; and

      • With respect to the disbursement for travelling and  gasoline,  the
        excess  per  diems  be  refunded  and  the  lacking  and  necessary
        corrective/explanatory  documents,  if  any,   be   submitted   for
        evaluation. Otherwise, the same may be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure  timely
      submission of accounts to facilitate the audit of the transactions  as
      well as to avoid the possible suspension or disallowance of  the  same
      and the imposition of administrative disciplinary sanctions.

    prescribed  under  COA  Circular  No.  2012-002.   Correspondingly,   the
    unsubmitted reports for 2016 should be  submitted  for  verification  and
    validation.

We reiterated the recommendation that the Local Chief  Executive
   require the LGU GAD Focal Person to:  (1)  prepare  an  Annual  GAD  Plan
   and Budget as well as the corresponding  Accomplishment  Report  for  the

We recommended that management prioritize the provision of a  sanitary

1.5	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to cancel the unclaimed stale checks and furnish the Accounting Office with the original DV and its supporting documents to be attached to the JEV for the recording of the necessary adjustments on the cash and liability accounts.

2.6      We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive:
direct the Inventory Committee of the Municipality to conduct a complete physical count of PPE and submit the required inventory report on time for the audit team’s assessment and validation;
require the designated Property Custodian and the Acting Municipal Accountant to maintain complete and updated property cards and ledger cards, respectively, for reconciliation and control purposes; and
instruct the Acting GSO to label all properties/ equipment, showing complete information/description for easy identification and monitoring.

3.4 	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor spearhead to a) stop the practice of paying various expenses through reimbursement basis; and (b) strictly adhere to the requirement of rules and regulations that all payments must be made by check directly to the suppliers.

4.5	We recommended for the third consecutive year that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer, who is in charge of the application and renewal of bonds to the Bureau of Treasury, to process/submit the application of bond of the concerned GSO immediately.

5.5	We recommended for the third consecutive year that management review the projects/ programs under the 20% Economic Development Fund and ensure that the provisions of Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 are strictly complied with and projects undertaken partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures. Continued disregard of the provisions may force the Audit Team to issue the necessary audit suspension/s or disallowance/s, as the case may warrant.

6.7		We recommended that management strictly a) consider proper estimation in procuring standby goods for relief operations in order to avoid spoilage; and b) comply with the provisions of the abovementioned regulations.

7.4	We recommended that a) management ensure that claims against government funds be supported with the required documents provided under the abovementioned regulations; and (b) the Acting Municipal Accountant make sure that the transactions are supported with complete documentation before certifying and effecting payment in order to avoid suspension and /or disallowance of the transactions.

8.13	We recommended that the Local School Board:
prepare a balanced budget with conscious and concerted efforts of observing compliance with the legal and regulatory requirements on SEF budgeting, allocation and utilization and refrain from accommodating expenditures for activities which are administrative in nature or not within the priority programs enumerated by the abovementioned laws, rules and regulations; and
coordinate with the Property Offices of both the LGU and the DepEd to strengthen the controls on property accountability to monitor the clearances of those of the DepEd personnel who are retiring, resigning or terminating their employment.

9.4	 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Budget Officer and the GAD Focal Point to comply with the guidelines set forth in the DBM-NEDA-NCRFW Joint Circular No. 2004-1 and the PCW/ DILG/ DBM/ NEDA Joint Circular No. 2013-01 to ensure the proper budgeting and utilization of the GAD Fund.

10.5	We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and the ICO-Municipal Treasurer to (a) strictly comply with the regulation on the rendition of accounts and financial reports in order not to delay the verification and post-audit activities; and (b) observe the due dates of submission of the required financial reports and documents to the Audit Team.

11.16	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) coordinate the efforts of public and private sectors and implement the five basic environmental laws, especially the establishment of a Materials Recovery Facility, on preserving and protecting the environment and public health as required under R.A. No. 9003. We further recommended that the LCE encourage the full participation of the barangays in the implementation of the law.

12.8	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC and Municipal Accountant to require the Supplier/Contractor to post the necessary warranty security in any acceptable form for implemented infrastructure projects, as prescribed under Section 62 of the IRR-A of RA No. 9184, one year after project completion or before the issuance of the certificate of final acceptance in order to assure just compensation in case of faulty or defective projects within the set period.

13.6	We recommended that Management strictly comply with the requirements on the submission to COA of contracts/ purchase orders and supporting documents within five days from its perfection/approval.

14.7	We recommended that members of the Sangguniang Bayan a) initiate the conduct of a thorough study of the roads existing within the Municipality and determine the availability of alternative routes; and b) enact an ordinance which will provide an exception to the existing prohibition of three-wheeled public conveyances operating along the national highways within the Municipality.
16.2	We recommended that the Municipality continuously adhere to R.A. No. 8291.

We recommended that, in terms of improving future campaigns for community and local government awareness of the Farm to Market Road (FMR) Project, the LGU should be more aggressive and systematic in the conduct of campaign activities to ensure effectiveness of the campaigns and to ensure that residents residing within the vicinity of proposed FMR projects are made fully aware of the fact that the LGU will be implementing or is implementing a construction project of farm to market road therein and thereby become cognizant not only of the location where the said FMR will be constructed but also as regards the potential benefits that will accrue to their community as a result thereof. Such awareness campaigns should be collaborated with the barangay officials concerned.
We likewise recommended continued conformity with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2013-004 which requires public notification of infrastructure projects through the posting of relevant information thereof on tarpaulin signboards suitably framed for outdoor display at the project location and posted as soon as the award for the project has been made. Consider also the recommendations given by the survey respondents, as follows: a) extend, widen and improve the FMR; b) Construct FMR projects in other areas of the locality to facilitate transport of projects; c) provide proper maintenance for the project; and d) maximize the use of fund for the project.
